postconditions: Behavior is represented as composable word definitions.

source_ref.quote_fragment: Everything in Forth is a word.

embedding_text: Forth treats behavior as words; design quality depends on defining small composable words rather than large procedures.

preconditions: A larger behavior can be split into independent steps.
postconditions: Each step becomes a reusable, testable word.

source_ref.quote_fragment: Having a larger set of simpler words makes it easy to use a technique we’ll call factoring.

embedding_text: Factoring means splitting behavior into many small words to improve reuse, testability, and change isolation.

preconditions: Words document consumed and produced stack values.
postconditions: Data moves implicitly between words with minimal call overhead.

source_ref.quote_fragment: Forth uses a stack for passing data.

embedding_text: In Forth, words communicate through the data stack; explicit parameter plumbing is reduced and composition becomes direct.

postconditions: Public words describe component capabilities; internals stay hidden.

source_ref.quote_fragment: We’ll call the set of words which describe a component a lexicon.

embedding_text: A component lexicon is the externally visible set of words that models what the component does while hiding implementation details.

preconditions: A single word mixes many concerns.
postconditions: Low reuse, difficult testing, difficult refactoring.

source_ref.quote_fragment: Forth encourages more words than a conventional program would be into subroutines.

embedding_text: Avoid large monolithic words; they reduce the primary advantage of Forth, which is aggressive factoring into composable units.

preconditions: Internal helper words are used externally.
postconditions: External code depends on internals and breaks under refactor.

source_ref.quote_fragment: internal words support the externally visible lexicon.

embedding_text: Do not expose internal support words as API; only lexicon words should be considered public interface.

preconditions: GETC leaves value c on stack.
TRANSFORM-TO-D consumes c and leaves d.
postconditions: Pipeline executes without explicit argument passing variables.
embedding_text: Example pipeline: GETC TRANSFORM-TO-D PUT-D demonstrates pure stack-mediated data flow and composition.

preconditions: Each word in a composed phrase has documented stack effect.
postconditions: Net stack effect matches intended interface and leaves no garbage.

source_ref.quote_fragment: provided ... the word doesn’t consume or leave any unexpected values.

embedding_text: Validation rule: composed words must maintain expected net stack effect; unexpected consumption or leftovers indicate a defect.

preconditions: Interactive system can compile and run words immediately.
postconditions: Design-test-refine loops happen in seconds or minutes.

source_ref.quote_fragment: turnaround on the order of seconds.

embedding_text: Forth development is iterative: define small words, test immediately, and refine continuously with fast feedback.

preconditions: Invocation mechanics and data passing are standardized by language model.
postconditions: Code emphasizes domain semantics rather than call boilerplate.

source_ref.quote_fragment: The resulting code becomes a concentrated interplay of words.

embedding_text: Forth removes call and parameter ceremony so code can encode domain meaning as a compact interplay of words.
